back: Pricing Calculator: estimate cost of a planned NEW deployment. TCO Calculator: compare EXISTING on-prem costs against running the same thing in Azure.
Portal tool for monitoring spend, setting budgets, and cost alerts. Alerts notify but don't auto-stop resources by themselves.
Reuse existing on-prem Windows Server/SQL Server licenses (with Software Assurance) to reduce Azure costs.
Pre-commit to 1 or 3 years of usage for a significant discount vs. pay-as-you-go pricing.
Outbound (egress) data transfer out of Azure is typically billed; inbound data transfer in is usually free.
Enforces resource CONFIGURATION/compliance rules (e.g. 'must use HTTPS'). Can audit, deny, or remediate non-compliant resources.
RBAC controls WHO can do WHAT. Azure Policy controls resource CONFIGURATION regardless of who makes the change. They're complementary.
CanNotDelete: blocks deletion, allows modification. ReadOnly: blocks both. Override even Owner-level RBAC permissions.
Packages policies, RBAC assignments, and resource templates together to quickly deploy a compliant environment to new subscriptions.
Discovers, classifies, and governs sensitive data across an organization, and assesses regulatory compliance risk.
Where Microsoft publishes official compliance certifications (ISO, SOC, GDPR, HIPAA) and audit documentation for Azure.

front: Azure Portal vs. CLI/PowerShell vs. ARM templates
back: Portal: GUI, one-off tasks. CLI/PowerShell: scripting. ARM/Bicep templates: declarative Infrastructure as Code for repeatable deployments.
Browser-based shell in the Azure Portal, pre-configured with CLI and PowerShell. Needs an Azure Files share to persist session data.
The underlying management/deployment layer every Azure tool (Portal, CLI, PowerShell, SDKs) goes through.
Extends Azure management (Policy, RBAC, monitoring) to resources running outside Azure — on-prem, other clouds, or the edge.
Personalized recommendations across five categories: Cost, Security, Reliability, Operational Excellence, Performance.

front: Azure Service Health vs. Azure Status
back: Service Health: personalized alerts for YOUR resources/regions. Azure Status: public, global dashboard for all Azure services.
Multiply the individual SLAs of dependent services together — the combined SLA is generally lower than any single component's SLA.
99.95% × 99.99% ≈ 99.94% — lower than either service's individual SLA.
Private Preview (invite-only, no SLA) → Public Preview (open, opt-in, usually no SLA) → General Availability (production-ready, SLA applies).
Basic: free, billing only. Developer/Standard/Professional Direct/Premier: paid, progressively faster technical support response times.
AI systems should treat all people fairly and avoid discriminatory bias against any group.
Being open about how an AI system works and disclosing when someone is interacting with AI rather than a human.
The people who design and deploy AI systems remain responsible for their outcomes — never 'the AI's fault.'
AI systems should work for people of all backgrounds, abilities, and life experiences.
AI systems should protect user data and be secure against misuse or breach.
AI systems should perform consistently and safely under the conditions they were designed for.
Defining infrastructure declaratively in a file (ARM template, Bicep) so it can be deployed consistently and repeatably.
Resource type, region, consumption/usage, and network egress traffic all affect what you're billed.
12 months of limited free services, a set of always-free services (with usage caps), plus a 30-day starting credit.

front: Governance vs. compliance vs. security tools
back: Azure Policy/Blueprints = governance. Microsoft Purview = data compliance. Microsoft Defender for Cloud/Sentinel = security posture and threat response.
Free and included with every subscription, but covers only billing/subscription management — not technical troubleshooting.
